steps.title: Identify what the researchers' conclusion requires
steps.content:
The researchers conclude that children may be more aware of mammals than of other animals. Supporting data must show mammals appearing in more drawings than birds and insects — not a comparison between birds and insects alone.
  • Choice A — 'There were about as many drawings with an example of an insect as there were with an example of a bird.': Compares only insects and birds — says nothing about mammals. Cannot support a claim about mammal awareness. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The fewest number of drawings contained an example of a bird.': The graph shows insects (~55%) appeared in fewer drawings than birds (~69%), so this misreads the data. ✗
  • Choice C — 'More of the drawings had an example of an insect than an example of a mammal.': The opposite of the data — mammals appear in ~80% of drawings, insects in ~55%. This would actually undermine the claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'More of the drawings had an example of a mammal than either an example of a bird or an example of an insect.': Mammals (~80%) exceed birds (~69%) and insects (~55%), directly showing children drew mammals more — supporting heightened awareness. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The researchers' claim is that children are more aware of mammals than of other animals. The data must show mammals outpacing both other groups — exactly what choice D states. That matches choice D — D is correct.
